name: Eta HTTP/1.1 server tail latency over real sockets

description: Baseline: Eta H1 server tail latency over real sockets (oha HTTP/1.1, 16 keep-alive conns, n=40000, median of 3). p99 geomean 221µs; static_1k highest (300µs).

asi.hypothesis: baseline of Eta H1 server tail latency over real sockets (oha HTTP/1.1, 16 keep-alive connections, n=40000, median of 3)
asi.next: Apply the proven H2 playbook. Biggest expected lever: per-request request-head-read Fiber.first + with_timeout in h1_server_connection.ml (~1054, ~1093) -- mirror the H2 handler-switch + per-request-fiber removal. Then handler/write/body timeouts -> per-connection watchdog (H2 #4/#5), and gate request_metrics on metrics_enabled (H2 #8).
asi.regime_note: H1 absolute latencies are ~10x lower than H2 (p99 geomean 221us vs H2 2540us) because H1 has no stream multiplexing contention -- each of the 16 connections handles one request at a time. rps_geomean 85k (lower than H2 164k since no multiplexing). Noise floor will be smaller in absolute us but likely similar in relative terms.
asi.tail_profile: p99 geomean 221us. static_1k highest p99 (300us, body-bearing). root lowest (166us). p99/p50 ratios: root 1.39x, user_id 1.31x, static 1.29x, echo 1.31x -- much tighter than H2 (2-3.3x), so H1 tail is already less spiky; gains likely come more from reducing per-request fixed overhead (forks, allocs) than from GC pause reduction.

description: H1 run_handler: replace per-request Fiber.first + with_timeout with per-connection watchdog + Cancel.sub (handler_watch slot). p99 geomean -9.2%, p50 -12.8%, rps +11.9%

asi.hypothesis: H1 run_handler did a per-request Eio.Fiber.first (fork fiber + cancel ctx + promise to race closed_signal) AND a per-request Eio.Time.with_timeout (sleeper fiber + Zzz node) for handler_timeout. Replace both with the proven H2 watchdog pattern: per-connection watchdog daemon + a single Eio.Cancel.sub per request registered in t.handler_watch; mark_closed cancels it on close, watchdog cancels on deadline. Distinguish close vs timeout by t.closed.
asi.next: Continue the H1 playbook: (1) read_request_head_with_timeout still arms a per-request with_timeout (~1093) -- replace with watchdog/deadline; (2) gate request_metrics + connection_metrics on Eta.Runtime.metrics_enabled (~1018, ~1212); (3) per-response micro-opts. Re-profile to confirm the new hot path.
asi.result: p99 geomean 220.77->200.47 (-9.2%, clears noise floor). All endpoints improved: root p99 166->139, user_id 234->206, static 300->280, echo 204->201. Low-noise confirmations: p50_geomean 166->145 (-12.8%), rps_geomean 85229->95387 (+11.9%). RSS flat 31MB.
asi.validation: all H1+shared unit suites pass (checks 3.5s). Semantics preserved: handler_timeout still enforced via watchdog (deadline cancel -> Eio.Time.Timeout -> handler_timeout_error); connection close still aborts in-flight handler (mark_closed cancels the cancel ctx; t.closed distinguishes -> connection_closed_error). Both still force_close=true as before. The per-request fiber fork + sleeper fork are gone.

description: H1: gate request_metrics + connection_metrics on Eta.Runtime.metrics_enabled (not just enable_otel). Skips per-request metric-attr building when no meter. p99 -2.5%, rps +4%

asi.hypothesis: H1 request_metrics + connection_metrics gated only on enable_otel (not metrics_enabled), so Server_metrics.request built Semconv attrs (string_of_int + list alloc) per request even though the probe runtime has no meter. Gate both on Eta.Runtime.metrics_enabled (H2 #8 analog).
asi.next: read_request_head_with_timeout (~1093) still arms per-request with_timeout for header/idle timeout -- but it guards a genuinely-blocking read (waiting for next request), so sync-skip doesn't apply directly; consider a watchdog-style read deadline. Then per-response micro-opts (single-chunk body, content-length decimal writer in response_write.ml). Re-profile to find the new H1 hot path.
asi.result: p99 geomean 200.47->195.40 (-2.5%, -11.5% from baseline). p50_geomean 145->140 (-3.4%), rps_geomean 95387->99214 (+4.0%, closing the Go 0.81x gap). RSS flat. All endpoints improved or flat.
asi.validation: all H1+shared unit suites pass. Zero behavior change for real OTEL users: a noop meter records nothing, so skipping attr building when metrics_enabled=false is observationally identical.

description: H1: build-time tracing branch in safe_handler_effect (skip per-request is_tracing_enabled Bind node). Discarded: neutral (p50/rps flat, p99 within noise) — Eta interpreter node overhead is below the noise floor.

asi.hypothesis: Skip the shared Tracer.request's per-request `is_tracing_enabled |> bind` wrapper by branching at build-time on Eta.Runtime.tracing_enabled in H1 safe_handler_effect (we have the runtime synchronously, unlike the shared tracer).
asi.finding: The Eta effect machinery is ALREADY lean. eval is a plain recursive GADT interpreter (Pure->ok immediately); effect_of_public/erase_runtime_error are %identity/Obj.magic (zero cost); with_finalizers is try/match (cheap when empty); the noop tracer's with_task_context is `fun _ f -> f ()`. Removing one per-request Bind node + closure produced NO measurable change (p50 + rps flat). So the per-request Eta node overhead is below the noise floor. The profile's caml_runstack/caml_alloc_stack_bind (~6%) is Eio's effect-based fiber scheduler (suspend/resume on socket I/O), NOT Eta -- and is inherent to Eio.
asi.rollback_reason: p99 geomean 195.40->203.53 (within body-endpoint noise; p50_geomean flat 140.32->140.22, rps flat 99214->97921). Neutral change that ADDS code (duplicates suppress/tracing branch logic already in Tracer.request) for no gain -> discard.
asi.next_action_hint: Eta machinery is not the bottleneck. Remaining H1 levers are framework-bound (Eio scheduler) or small per-response allocs. Consider: read-head with_timeout->watchdog (one more per-request fork), or accept H1 is near its floor (already -11.5% p99 / +16% rps from baseline, closing the Go gap). Do NOT pursue Eta-interpreter micro-opts -- proven below noise.

description: H1 read-head: replace per-request with_timeout with the per-connection watchdog (handler_watch slot, generalized poll). p99 geomean -11.7%, p50 -11.6%, rps +11%

asi.hypothesis: read_request_head_with_timeout armed a per-request Eio.Time.with_timeout (sleeper fiber + Zzz node) on every kept-alive request-head read. Replace with the per-connection watchdog: register a read deadline + Cancel.sub in handler_watch (read and handler never overlap in the sequential run_requests loop), generalize watchdog_loop to run if handler/request_header/idle timeout is set and poll on the min/8.
asi.next: All three per-request forks now removed (handler #2, read-head #5) + metrics gate (#3). Per the user: stack OxCaml microopts (local_/stack_ allocation, [@inline] hot paths, portability annotations) on the H1 hot path -- frame.ml/parse/response_write/server_request. Eta interpreter confirmed lean (#4). Re-profile to find the new top cost.
asi.result: p99 geomean 195.40->172.53 (-11.7%, -21.9% from baseline). All endpoints improved: root 131->124, user_id 197->183, static 269->252, echo 210->155. p50_geomean 140->124 (-11.6%), rps_geomean 99214->110161 (+11.0%). RSS flat 31MB.
asi.validation: all H1+shared unit suites pass (checks 3.4s). Semantics: head-read timeout still enforced (watchdog cancels the read's Cancel.sub at deadline -> Timeout). Close during idle-wait aborts the read via mark_closed cancelling handler_watch. watchdog now runs if any of handler/request_header/idle timeout is set.

description: H1 response write: replace per-write with_timeout with per-connection watchdog (handler_watch slot). Fires on head+body of every response. p99 geomean -15.8%, p50 -19.7%, rps +23.8%

asi.hypothesis: write_response_wire still armed a per-write Eio.Time.with_timeout (sleeper fiber + Zzz node) for response_write_timeout -- and a fixed response does >=2 writes (head + body), so >=2 forks per response on every endpoint. Route through the per-connection watchdog: register write deadline + Cancel.sub in handler_watch (writes are sequential after the handler, slot is free). Found via reading the write path while investigating memtrace.
asi.memtrace_findings: mtop CTF analysis: top per-request Eta alloc is Response_write.prepare Buffer.create 256 (3.3%); static_1k handler reads the file from disk per request (~35%, OFF-LIMITS testsuite handler); Eio scheduler internals Psq/Lwt_dllist/Cancel (~8%, inherent); per-connection Cstruct buffers (not per-request). The write-path with_timeout was found by code-reading, not memtrace (it's CPU/scheduler cost, not alloc).
asi.next: Consider combining head+body into one writev (currently 2 Eio.Flow.write per fixed response -> 2 syscalls). Response_write.prepare Buffer reuse (3.3% alloc, but alloc isn't the H1 latency bottleneck). Re-profile to confirm the write-fork is gone.
asi.result: p99 geomean 172.53->145.35 (-15.8%, -34.2% from baseline). All endpoints dropped: root 124->107, user_id 183->151, static 252->221, echo 155->125. Low-noise confirmations: p50_geomean 124->99 (-19.7%), rps_geomean 110161->136428 (+23.8%). RSS 31->30MB.
asi.validation: all H1+shared unit suites pass. Semantics: write timeout still enforced (watchdog cancels write's Cancel.sub at deadline -> Cancelled Timeout -> response_write_error when not closed). Close during write (mark_closed cancels, t.closed=true) propagates via `Cancelled _ -> raise` to unwind, as before. All write_response_wire callers (head, body chunks, 100-continue, error responses) now use the watchdog uniformly.

description: H1: write response head+body in a single writev (write_response_head_and_body) instead of separate head/body writes. Halves write syscalls + watchdog arming. p99 -16.6%, rps +24%

asi.hypothesis: A fixed response did 2 Eio.Flow.write calls (head, then body chunks) = 2 writev syscalls + 2 watchdog Cancel.sub arming per response. Combine head + all body chunks into a single writev (blit all into the per-connection write_buffer, one watched write) via write_response_head_and_body.
asi.next: rps_geomean now 170k (+99% from baseline). H1 Eta should be well ahead of Go now. Remaining: Response_write.prepare Buffer.create per response (memtrace 3.3%); the request record + lazy id alloc per request; or re-run the server-load comparison to confirm the Go gap is closed. Diminishing returns -- consider finalizing H1 soon.
asi.result: p99 geomean 145.35->121.18 (-16.6%, -45.1% from baseline). user_id p99 151->106 (single-chunk body, went from 2 writes to 1 -- biggest beneficiary), root 107->106, static 221->161, echo 125->119. p50_geomean 99->78 (-21.8%), rps_geomean 136428->169774 (+24.4%, +99% from baseline). RSS 30->29.8MB.
asi.validation: all H1+shared unit suites pass. Correctness: head+body serialized in order into write_buffer (head at offset 0, chunks appended); No_body and streaming paths unchanged (head-only write then stream); metrics (response_body_bytes) still emitted with body_len. write_response_wire grows the buffer if head+body exceeds capacity.

description: H1: reuse per-connection Buffer for response-head serialization (?buf to prepare). Discarded: below p99 noise floor (p50/rps flat) — allocation is not the H1 latency bottleneck.

asi.hypothesis: Reuse a per-connection Buffer for response-head serialization (memtrace's top addressable per-request Eta alloc: Response_write.prepare Buffer.create 256, 3.7%) via an optional ?buf param to prepare, threading t.response_buffer.
asi.finding: CONFIRMS the session pattern: allocation reduction below ~3-4% is below the H1 p99 noise floor. The big H1 wins (#2/#5/#6/#7: p99 -45%, rps +99%) ALL came from removing per-op Eio forks (with_timeout/Fiber.first) and halving write syscalls -- CPU/scheduler costs, NOT heap allocation. RSS held ~30MB throughout; GC is not the H1 bottleneck at these tiny latencies (~120us p99). memtrace was invaluable for ruling OUT allocation as the lever and confirming the remaining big allocators are off-limits (static_1k handler file-read) or per-connection/framework.
asi.rollback_reason: p99 geomean 121.18->129.69 (within noise; user_id p99 outlier 106->142). p50_geomean flat (77.65->76.77), rps_geomean -3.5% (within noise). Pure alloc reduction that cannot raise latency -> the delta is noise. Adds a ?buf API param for no measurable gain -> discard.
